title: The Knees and the Instinct to Collapse or Run
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: The body's memory of trauma is held in the knees, creating an instinct to either run or collapse. Learn how to heal this internal conflict.
imageDescription: Golden light illuminates the intricate grain of a wooden floor, where a pair of bare feet are firmly and gently planted. The scene evokes a feeling of profound grounding, safety, and the quiet strength of returning home to the body. The light is warm and forgiving, casting soft shadows.
htmlContent:
The body doesn't keep score. It is the score. Every sensation, every tension, every involuntary brace is a word in a language far older than thought, a direct communication from the deepest parts of our organismic intelligence.

The Body’s Ancient Language of Threat

We are a culture entranced by narrative, conditioned to believe that memory is a function of the mind, a vast and searchable library of filed-away stories and images that we can, with some effort, retrieve and inspect. But this is a profoundly incomplete picture. The oldest stories, the ones that shape our perception of the world from the inside out, are not stored in the language of words but in the language of pure sensation, in the deep, pre-verbal archives of the body. This isn’t a poetic metaphor; it is the sober reality of our physiological inheritance. This is the realm of procedural memory, the memory of how to do things—how to ride a bicycle, how to brace for an impact, how to shrink in the face of anger. It is a memory held not in the cerebral cortex but in the cerebellum, the brainstem, and the very tissues of the body. Long before the thinking brain evolved its intricate capacity for storytelling, the nervous system was already mastering a far more fundamental grammar—the grammar of survival. And much of that language is written in the muscles, bones, and connective tissues of the lower body, the structures designed to carry us toward safety or hold us in the suspended animation of a threat too large to fight or flee.

The hips, the powerful flexors of the legs, and most profoundly, the knees, serve as the engine of our most primal survival responses. The knees, elegant in their biomechanical complexity, are designed for both rapid mobilization and sudden, complete immobilization. They are the hinge upon which the decision to run, to stand firm, or to collapse is physically enacted. When we explore the territory of complex trauma, we are not merely excavating psychological wounds but are instead learning to read the text of the body itself, a text written in the ink of adrenaline and cortisol. In my years working in this territory, I've seen how the body tells a story the person cannot yet put into words. The story is often one of a profound and unresolved conflict between two of the nervous system’s most powerful and ancient directives: the instinct to run for one’s life and the instinct to collapse in on oneself to survive an overwhelming force. This is not a story of past events, but a living, breathing reality unfolding in the present moment, in the felt sense of the body itself.

When 'Run' Becomes an Internal Marathon

For many who have navigated the landscape of complex trauma, the flight response is not a singular event in the past but a chronic, ongoing condition of the present. The original threat may be decades gone, but the command to “run” continues to echo through the nervous system, a silent and exhausting internal marathon. This emerges not as a conscious desire to sprint from the room, but as a pervasive and deeply unsettling restlessness, an inability to truly settle into the moment, into one’s own skin. It is the low-hum of activation that feels like a constant, inexplicable need to be somewhere else, a motor that is always running, burning fuel even when the vehicle is parked. It's the fidgeting foot, the drumming fingers, the constant need to be busy, to be productive, to be moving, lest the stillness allow something far more uncomfortable to surface. This is the signature of a flight response that was initiated but never completed, never brought to a resolution of safety.

This internal state has a direct physical correlate. It lives as a subtle but persistent tension in the large muscles of the legs—the quads, the hamstrings, the calves—a feeling of being perpetually poised on the balls of the feet, ready to launch into a sprint at any moment. The world is perceived not as a place of potential rest, but as a track, and we are forever in the starting blocks. The work of researchers like Pat Ogden, the founder of sensorimotor psychotherapy, has been pivotal in illuminating this territory. Ogden’s genius was in recognizing that the body must be included in the therapeutic process, that trauma recovery often involves helping the body to finally complete the defensive actions that were thwarted or frozen long ago. That powerful, life-saving impulse to run, when denied its physical expression, doesn’t simply vanish; it becomes a source of profound and persistent dysregulation, a ghost in the machine. We are tasked not with exorcising this ghost, but with understanding its origins, with feeling its energy, and with gently, slowly, guiding it toward a sense of completion that was not available in the past. It is about turning toward the very sensations we have been running from for a lifetime, and discovering that they are not the threat we believed them to be.

Awareness doesn't need to be cultivated. It needs to be uncovered.

The Gravity of Collapse

At the opposite pole of the survival spectrum from the frantic energy of flight lies the profound stillness of collapse. This is not the gentle yielding of restorative rest; it is a sudden, involuntary descent into a state of energetic and physical implosion, a shutdown of the system when the threat is deemed inescapable. It is the body’s oldest and most extreme survival strategy, a feigned death that is the nervous system’s last resort when fight and flight are no longer viable options. This state is often experienced as a sudden, crushing wave of heaviness, a feeling that the limbs have turned to lead, and a sense that the world is receding, becoming distant and muffled. The knees, in this instance, do not tremble with the readiness to run but buckle and give way under the sheer weight of the imperative to disappear. This is not a choice. It is a biological imperative, as automatic and non-negotiable as a reflex.

To frame the buckling of the knees as a moment of weakness is to fundamentally misunderstand the intelligence of the body. It is a highly sophisticated, energy-conserving response designed to maximize the chances of survival in the face of overwhelming and inescapable threat. It is the body wisely pulling its own plug. In the language of polyvagal theory, this is the activation of the dorsal vagal complex, the unmyelinated, ancient branch of the vagus nerve that slams the brakes on the metabolic engine, inducing a state of immobilization that can look like depression, apathy, or chronic fatigue. For those with a history of complex trauma, this dorsal state can become a familiar, almost gravitational pull, a retreat from a world that feels too demanding, too activating, too unsafe. Learning about understanding the nervous system's hierarchy of response is the first step in recognizing that this collapse is not a character flaw, not a moral failing, but a physiological state with a deep and intelligent history. It is the body’s wisdom, even if it feels like a prison.

The Knees as a Crossroads of Intention

The knees, in the context of a traumatized nervous system, become a physical and metaphorical crossroads, the precise anatomical location where the body’s most contradictory intentions collide. They are the battlefield where the sympathetic nervous system’s desperate scream to “run!” meets the dorsal vagal system’s profound, gravity-like pull to “collapse!” One part of the organism is flooring the accelerator while another is simultaneously slamming on the brakes. The result is not stillness, but a state of highly activated and yet frozen immobility, a physiological stalemate that is profoundly distressing and deeply confusing to the person experiencing it. It is the energy of a panic attack, trapped inside a body that is simultaneously shutting down.

The felt sense of this internal conflict is one of trembling, of a deep and unnerving weakness in the legs, a sense of being utterly unable to stand and yet incapable of truly resting. The knees may literally shake, caught between the opposing commands to mobilize and immobilize. This is the state that so many describe as feeling “stuck,” and it is not a psychological metaphor but a physiological reality. The body is trying to do two opposite things at the exact same time, and the knees are the epicenter of this neurological civil war. This state is often misdiagnosed as generalized anxiety, or a mood disorder, because its origins in the body's survival system are not understood. This is the territory where the practice of somatic experiencing becomes so vital, as it offers a way to begin to listen to these opposing signals not as a problem to be solved, but as a conversation to be witnessed. It is about bringing a gentle, non-judgmental curiosity to the trembling, to the weakness, to the profound sense of being caught between worlds, and allowing the body to tell its story in its own time, at its own pace.

The algorithm of your attention determines the landscape of your experience.

Reclaiming the Right to Stand, and the Right to Rest

The journey of integration and healing from this physiological schism is not about choosing one response over the other, not about valorizing the impulse to run or condemning the impulse to collapse. It is not about trying to eliminate these powerful, life-saving responses from our repertoire. Instead, the work is about slowly and patiently building the capacity within the nervous system to experience these energetic tides without being completely swept away by them. It is about expanding our ability to be with the activation of flight without it becoming a full-blown panic attack, and to be with the heaviness of collapse without it becoming a multi-day shutdown. The path forward lies in the art of titration and pendulation, of touching into these difficult states for brief, manageable moments and then returning to a sense of relative safety and resource in the present. It is a gentle oscillation between the charge of the past and the ground of the present.

This is the work of reclaiming two fundamental human rights that are so often stolen by trauma: the right to stand and the right to rest. The right to stand means feeling a sense of solidity in one’s own legs, a feeling of being grounded and present in the world, capable of meeting its challenges with a sense of agency. The right to rest means being able to yield to stillness and softness without that surrender being a trauma-induced collapse. It is the difference between choosing to lie down and being taken down by the weight of unprocessed history. This reclamation happens through micro-movements, through the gentle, exploratory work of feeling one’s feet on the floor, of noticing the support of the ground coming up to meet you, of allowing a small, intentional tremble without it cascading into a full-body tremor. It is a slow, deliberate process of re-inhabiting the lower body, of making it a safe and trustworthy place to reside. It is, in essence, a guide to somatic grounding, a journey back to the earth beneath our feet, one sensation at a time.

What the Research Cannot Capture

Ultimately, this deep work with the body’s survival responses is not about fixing something that is broken. The nervous system is not a faulty machine in need of repair. It is a profoundly intelligent and adaptive system that has done exactly what it needed to do to ensure our survival. The work, then, is one of relationship-building. It is about moving from a state of alienation from these powerful, ancient, and often terrifying parts of our own physiology to a state of intimacy and communication with them. It is a process of learning to listen to the stories the body is telling, not with an agenda of changing them, but with the simple, open-hearted intention of understanding them. This is how we begin to build a nervous system that has more choices, a system that is not locked into the binary of “run” or “collapse,” but has access to a whole spectrum of nuanced and creative responses to life. It's like learning the language of a country you were born in but never taught how to speak.

This is the architecture of returning. It is the slow, patient, and often non-linear process of re-inhabiting a body that we may have long ago abandoned out of necessity. It is about discovering that the sensations we have feared for a lifetime are not the enemy, but are in fact the gateway to a deeper and more authentic connection to ourselves and to the world. It is a journey from the abstract realm of psychological concepts to the living, breathing reality of the flesh. It is a path that requires courage, compassion, and a willingness to be with the profound mystery of our own embodiment. It is a journey that ultimately leads us to a place where the old, rigid distinctions between mind and body, between psychology and spirituality, begin to soften and dissolve into a more unified and integrated whole, a place where we can finally come to rest in the truth of our own experience.

At a certain depth of inquiry, the distinction between psychology and philosophy dissolves entirely.

The question is not whether you are ready. It is whether you are willing. Those are different things, and the difference matters more than you think.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my legs feel so weak and shaky when I'm anxious?

That sensation of weakness and shakiness in the legs during moments of high anxiety or fear is the direct physical outcome of a nervous system in conflict. Your sympathetic nervous system is activating the powerful flight response, sending a surge of energy to your legs to prepare you to run. Simultaneously, if the threat feels inescapable or overwhelming, your dorsal vagal system may begin to initiate a collapse or freeze response, which acts as a powerful brake. This collision of 'go' and 'stop' signals in your nervous system results in the trembling, weakness, or buckling sensation in your knees and legs. It's not a sign of actual physical weakness, but rather a sign of a system in a state of profound internal conflict.

Can I ever stop feeling this way?

The goal of this work is less about completely eliminating these feelings and more about building your capacity to experience them without being overwhelmed. These are, after all, essential survival instincts that are hardwired into our biology for a very good reason. The work is to widen your window of tolerance, so that these responses are triggered less frequently and feel less intense when they do arise. Over time, through consistent and gentle practice, the nervous system learns it has more options than just full-throttle acceleration or a complete shutdown, allowing for a greater sense of ease, resilience, and choice in your daily life. The aim is not to silence the body's signals, but to become a more skilled and compassionate listener.

The body was never against you. It was only ever for you, using the only tools it had in its arsenal to keep you alive. The return home is simply learning to listen to what it was saying all along, beneath the noise of the story you thought was your life.

For those interested in exploring the physiological imprint of trauma, the resources provided by the National Institute of Mental Health offer a comprehensive and scientifically grounded overview of the current understanding of how overwhelming experiences shape our biology.

Here is the uncomfortable truth: understanding changes nothing without practice.
